sentences.text: My name is Edith Abrahams.
And it's spelled A-B-R-A-H-A-M-S.
Edith Wolf, spelled W-O-L-F.
Nope, that's it.
I was born April 30, in 1920.
I was born in Wilhelmshaven, [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] means it's like a county in Germany.
It's the northern part of Germany.
Yeah.
I am.
My mother and dad were living in Wilhelmshaven, as I said.
But did you want to hear a little bit about my mother first?
My mother was an unusual kind of person.
My grandparents lived in Bad Nauheim.
It was a resort town.
They came from Galician.
It seems that my grandmother's whole family came to the United States, they immigrated.
And my grandmother met my grandfather, and she was the only one who went to Germany.
And when they came to Germany in Bad Nauheim, they opened a hotel.
And this was a Jewish hotel.
It was an orthodox hotel.
And my uncles, at that time, were young.
And my mother-- she was-- she was a little older-- and she was the one that had to help in that hotel.
She was an independent person, even at that time.
And what was unusual about her, that she was a woman had her own mind way before anyone else during that time ever did the things that she decided to do.
Because my grandmother had arranged a marriage for her, and she decided at the day that she was supposed to be married that she would not be married to this man, and she just left.
And everyone came to the wedding, except the bride.
And that was very unusual.
And my grandfather from my father's side-- this is a little later-- happened to be at the hotel and knew about this, which was a scandalous affair for this small town.
My mother did not care one way or the other.
And he was impressed with her spunk.
And since he had an eligible son, he took a photo and sent it to his son, which became my father.
He was not interested in arranged marriage either, but when he looked at my mother's picture-- she was a pretty girl-- he decided to go to Bad Nauheim and investigate.
And after two days, they became engaged.
And I found it very interesting that she never wanted to have an arranged marriage, and, more or less, this was an arranged marriage.
But they were extremely happy.
Now, when they first got married, they were in-- lived in Wilhelmshaven, and my father had opened a pawnshop, because, apparently, that was going on before then.
My grandfather had a pawnshop, too.
But my mother missed her family, because they had been in Frankfurt, and so they decided to come to Frankfurt.
And when I was one-year-old-- this when we moved to where the rest of the family was-- my-- I had two sisters, I was the youngest one.
I had-- my older sister's name was Renata.
And my middle sister's name was Margot.
The rest of the family on my mother's side, these were all there, were my mother had two brothers-- three brothers.
The oldest one was called Sally.
We would say this is an American name, it was a girl's name.
There, it was a German name.
Then she had a brother Jacob, which became Jack.
And she had another brother, his name was Wilhelm.
Wilhelm died in World War I.
He was my mother's favorite.
And the two brothers had different vocations entirely.
My uncle Sidney, he was a businessman.
And my uncle Jack had wonderful ability with his hands.
And he did all kinds of inventions.
And he became a dental technician.
But in Germany, dental technicians were permitted to practice dentistry and he became a dentist.
He became my dentist.
He married a charming woman from Latvia.
Her name was Annie.
And they had two boys-- Leo and Benno were their children.
My uncle Sally, he married a woman, her name was Hetta, and she had--
Actually, she had four brothers.
She had-- the oldest one was Moritz, and they lived in-- they moved to Wiesbaden.
This was a town nearby, too.
And this is exactly what our family was.
Part of it was Bad Nauheim, part of it was in Wiesbaden.
And the ones in Wiesbaden, he had a wife, and her name was Lilly.
And I'm going to come to her later, because that's important.
And they have two daughters-- Ruth and Helga.
And we visited them often.
We were pretty close.
But the early years, in the 1920s, we lived on the [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's when my parents did not have a pawnshop.
They had a leather goods store that they opened up.
And my father, who was quite a bright man, was asked by the Phoenix Insurance Company to come in, I think as an agent first, and then he became the director for the company.
He was a very valuable asset to the company.
And this was in the '20s.
Originally, we stayed in [NON-ENGLISH] for quite a while.
We had good friends in that area.
It's where I played.
We then moved to Chernivtsi, and I had my first taste of anti-Semitism.
I think I was 10.
I think it was in 1930.
This was just a-- it seems an innocent thing, but it wasn't to me.
Girls-- you know how girls skip rope, and we used to skip rope.
And we always sang these little ditties.
And it was in the cadence of jumping rope.
And this girl told this-- sang, and said no, let's do this one.
And she said-- and the words were so offensive to me-- would you want me to say them in German?
Sure.
It was called-- she said [SINGING IN GERMAN].
The translation is Jew, Jew, shit in a bag.
If you miss, you're automatically out.
I remembered that my face was getting very red.
I mean, I felt the heat.
I was so uncomfortable.
I took a look at the girl, I turned around.
I didn't say one word, and I went home.
And I waited until my dad got home and I was extremely upset and I told him.
And his only reaction was, well, you don't play with the kid anymore.
And that was the end of it.
But I felt the reaction should have been different, but, these things were not discussed.
Yes, my mother used to-- in fact, the household was different around Shabbat, around Sabbath.
You would come into the house from school on Friday and you could smell the freshly-baked challah.
And my mother would light the candles before the dinner.
She'd cover her hands over her eyes and she'd light the candles and I always thought it was very beautiful.
My father always, after every meal, he would say the prayer, which we called [NON-ENGLISH].
And this particular prayer, and I know the words by heart-- the ending of the prayer I learned later, that actually the last part of that is the same as it's said in the Kaddish.
And it's thanking God for what is given to you.
But in the Kaddish also, it's thanking God and even for something that is taken away from you.
It is just a-- an-- an acceptance of a thank you to God.
And it's what stayed with me all these years.
On religious life otherwise, my parents were not orthodox.
We did not keep the Sabbaths aside from Friday night.
But I did go to a Jewish school.
It was called the Philanthropin.
And the school existed from 1804 until 1942.
It was a very famous school.
The school in the city of Frankfurt, it served an important part of education.
In fact, the poet Goethe mentioned this in his literary writings.
He mentioned the school, so it was of importance.
Unfortunately, I wasn't very happy at the school.
I was unhappy with my teacher.
And eventually, my dad took me out of the school and I went to a private school, which was much more important to me.
And the school-- the private school was in the same building.
It was like an annex to the-- which is now considered a reformed synagogue.
And this was a more creative school and I was much happier there.
We belonged to a synagogue-- excuse me-- that was called the [NON-ENGLISH] Synagogue.
It was an important synagogue, but it was not the most important one in Frankfurt.
The most important synagogue there was the one, [NON-ENGLISH], which also has a history.
My grandparents went to that synagogue.
And I remember so well, because, for the high holidays, during that time, I would go-- we, the three girls, my two sisters and I-- would go and visit my grandparents.
We called them Oma and Opa.
Now, this was an orthodox synagogue.
It was a very large edifice.
And downstairs, the men were segregated from the women.
And my grandmother sat in the balcony.
She had the same seat, year after year.
And she would sit in the back.
She wore this lovely little lace, little kerchief on her head.
And then we would listen to the congregation.
They did not have a choir, but the whole congregation was very fluent in Hebrew.
And they would sing and chant together.
And it was really quite beautiful.
And I was little, and I had this-- must have had a vivid imagination because of a Rosh Hashanah, the shofar is blown to issue in the new year.
And I remembered that I thought it was gold.
I discovered later it surely wasn't gold, it's a ram's horn.
But I visualized it to be gold.
And I would stand on her seat.
She'd let me stand on her seat because I was too little.
You couldn't look over the banister.
It was an actual banister segregating the upstairs and the downstairs.
And I would look down to my Opa, and waited until he would turn around and look at us.
There was no talking to each other.
And that was very important to me.
Well, I went to a Jewish school, as I said, the Philanthropin.
And it was a secular school.
Their emphasis was on general education.
There was some-- obviously-- some Hebrew that was taught, but I cannot remember any of that.
I don't know why, but I can't.
But I am much more-- I was much more involved in the private school that I was in later.
But the school itself left an important part on my life because the girls that, you know, these were-- the boys and girls were in separate classes.
But the girls in my class were my friends.
In fact, later, there were two reunions in the United States.
And I was so excited meeting friends that I hadn't seen for over 50 years.
And that was pretty good.
Well, I had one.
And I liked her very much and I really thought she was my closest friend.
Her name was Margot Hesse.
But she was a disappointment to me at a very unexpected time.
And this was when Hitler had already come to power.
But first, I must-- just must mention before we come to her, a little bit about during the Hitler period.
Because we had moved to the-- a lot of things were changing.
We had moved to Chernivtsi and my parents had talked about the inflation and where the money was printed almost daily.
Their money was worthless.
And people were unhappy, and suddenly there were many different political parties.
And the-- it was called Nationalsozialistische Party, was one of them.
And it didn't seem to be anything.
I mean, my parents' friends, they laughed it off as a joke.

As a matter of fact, there was a-- in the early '20s, there was a joke going around-- I would say it was around 1928, when Hitler was just beginning-- it was the greeting Heil Hitler, which later was the only greeting around in Germany.
The joke was 'Heil' also means heal, which is the same thing.
In other words, heil the conqueror, but it's also another way of saying heil is healing someone.
So the joke would be, when someone would say Heil Hitler, and the answer would be why, is he sick.
I mean, it seems not an American kind of joke, but for Germans, at that time, they thought it was funny.
So the population in general did not love Hitler immediately.
This came much later.
But, for me, it brought it home in the early 1930s.
Like I mentioned that girl, that was antisemitism, and then this friend.
Because in 1932 is when the Hitler Youth was started.
And it never occurred to me that my closest friend would be part of the Hitler Youth.
Because I went to her house, and she'd come to my house, and she would go on excursions with me.
And there was no question.
It never even occurred to me that she'd be anything else that I am, you know.
Same kind of person.
But I was at her house and playfully sort of opened the drawers.
You know, we used to kid around and do that.
And I opened one of her drawers, and right on top, there was a Hitler uniform and I was shocked.
And I said to her, what is this.
And her manner changed immediately.
And she said to me, I was going to try and tell you this before.
Ed, I don't want you to come anymore.
You're a Jew.
And she used some very unpleasant things.
It wasn't like the same person I had known for quite a while.
She was really nasty to me.
And she said and you're hurting my father's business.
Her father was a shoemaker, not a shoe repairman.
He made-- he created shoes.
And he said-- you've gotta get out of here right now.
And it was very hard for me to accept that this, my friend, had literally gotten rid of me.
And now, to me, that was the beginning.
And we had another incident that was rather interesting, but it was a reverse kind of thing.
Because we had a carpenter, used to come to our house and used to do repairs and build things, and he was a very nice man.
He had a lovely wife.
We were not friendly with his wife, but he felt like he was part of our family.
He was not Jewish.
He'd come to our house, usually on a Sunday morning.
And he'd have-- he'd have coffee and talk, because he liked my dad.
And suddenly, he wasn't arriving anymore.
He had two sons.
He had two teenage sons.
And we never met the sons.
And he had been gone for about a month.
We couldn't imagine what was happening.
And my dad said, well, perhaps he's doing some work on Sundays.
And one Sunday morning, the doorbell rang, and he was standing there.
And he said I must talk to you.
And he came in and he started to cry.
And he said my own sons have turned me in.
They said that-- went to-- at that time, the Gestapo was already started.
And he said they had mentioned that I was fraternizing with a Jew.
And they actually brought him to jail.
He was there overnight.
He was interrogated.
And he said I was ashamed of my family, and we will never see each other again.
And to us, it was a terrible situation and brought home that things had changed very radically.
Oh, yes.
There, he had something that was quite scary.
When my father started with the Phoenix Insurance Company, he needed a car, and my father never drove.

And the company felt that it was not unusual to hire a chauffeur for the people that were working for them, especially if they had to travel, because they felt it was a waste for the director to drive and have to worry about a car.
So we had a chauffeur, which he was not for our family.
He was just for my dad.
We had this man for years.
And then something happened and we needed a new chauffeur.
And they had hired this man who had come to our house and picked up my dad and had been with us-- I don't know-- a couple of months or so.
And began to make anti-Jewish remarks, and very nasty remarks.
And my dad decided to fire him because he felt he couldn't use him.
At that point, he came to work the very next day with a revolver and he tried to kill my dad.
And luckily, some of the agents were there and they grabbed the gun and they got rid of him.
But it drove home some fear to us, which we had not expected.
And pretty soon after that, when Hitler was more established, was in 1932, was just before he became chancellor, the situation changed completely.
And the Phoenix Company, who were very pleased with my dad, had to let him go because he was Jewish.
And Jews were not allowed in positions anymore at that time.
They were beginning to remove the Jewish people from all corporate offices and other important positions.
The company liked my dad so well, they paid him one year severance pay, which was very unusual.
At that point, the physicians could no longer practice.
Lawyers could no longer practice.
It was slowly at first, and then it became a very rapid kind of movement.
And what happened then was that my dad had to look for something else.
And he decided to open a leather goods store, because that's something they'd had when they first came to Frankfurt.
He was experienced.
In 1933, a boycott was presented to all of Germany.
And in Frankfurt, of course, the boycott had to-- had to be done, too.
And I don't think I'll ever forget that day because it was in April of 1933, when I was there, when the boycott was going on.
I had left school and I was in the back room and doing homework, and my sister, my oldest sister, Renata, she was a sales girl.
And my sister Margot was in the back, too.
She was doing her homework, too.
I don't remember.
My dad was there.
I don't think there was anyone else.
Except, you know, at that time, we still had the [NON-ENGLISH], that means the [NON-ENGLISH].
In other words, the police were still there.
They had already had the SA Stormtrooper and the SS ones.
They were just beginning to exist.
But the police still was protecting the people.
The police had known my dad for many years and he knew us well.
But the-- since it was-- you know, the boycott was in existence, the SR had to stand in front of the stores.
And, of course, it was well-publicized.
And they had signs up, you know, that people shouldn't buy there because it was a Jewish store.
And if there wasn't a sign up, well, then someone had to station themselves in front of the store.
This stormtrooper had moved away and a woman had just come and was standing in front of the store when a customer had arrived inside our store.
And she had bought something.
She had disregarded the edict not to shop at a Jew's store.
And the woman who was standing in front was quiet.
And then the customer decided to leave.
She purchased something and she decided to leave.
And the woman in front of the store literally blocked the door.
My dad just went over and asked the woman very nicely to please move so the customer could leave.
And I don't even think he touched her.
He just talked to her.
She turned around and started to scream, the Jew has touched me.
Let's lynch the Jew.
There was a commotion in two seconds.
I remember, we came in towards the front of the store, and we were pretty frightened.
The customer, in the meanwhile, slipped away.
And the policeman that we knew came and said no one is going to lynch anyone.
And there was a large crowd in front of our store.
And he said, everyone go home.
And people dispersed.
The woman disappeared.
And he said to my dad, lock up and don't come back because I don't know what's going to be.
We had-- in the meanwhile, we had moved to a different section of Frankfurt.
We had moved across the River Main to a place called Sachsenhausen because we could no longer afford the apartment since my dad had lost his job.
We stayed in the apartment.
I mean, I don't mean we stayed there continually, you know.
We just didn't go back to the store.
The policeman came to us after, I think this was about three weeks, and said, I would suggest that you don't open that store again.
I have been to the Gestapo.
The Gestapo was established then.
The Gestapo was something like you would call the secret police.
He said, you are on the blacklist.
Blacklist means that if you don't leave, you will be going to jail.
I can assure you that's going to happen.
It may not be happening tomorrow, but it's going to happen.
So stay away from the store or do anything, but be careful.
So at that point, we decided that we had to leave Germany.
No, not in 1933.
In 19-- These things were subtle in 1933.
That was coming later.
What did happen, I forgot to tell you, the boycott did not just involve us.
It involved a great many things.
For example, I remember, on that same day that we had our incident, there were things that we didn't see, but they were in the newspaper because they loved to publicize it.
They took people off the street that they knew they looked Jewish.
For example, they had some people who were going to the Yeshiva, and, you know, they had the curls and the black hats.
So they look different and they picked them out immediately.
They had them scrub the gutter with toothbrushes.
And, of course, they took photographs and it was well-publicized.
Very shortly after that, they had the book burning at the Romer.
The Romer in Frankfurt is a very important landmark.
That was the city hall.
It was actually the residences of royalty in the early years in the city of Frankfurt.
It was important to me because I had been there with my sister just a little while before.
And it was just an important part of my life.
